editableNewDialogue.text: If the database can’t enforce the FK for that polymorphic @ManyToOne in TABLE_PER_CLASS, what does Hibernate actually put in the table? A single column? Multiple columns?

Typically it has to store (1) the referenced id value and (2) enough type information to know which concrete table/entity that id belongs to. With plain JPA @ManyToOne you normally get a single join column that points to one target table, but for a polymorphic target with TABLE_PER_CLASS there isn’t one table to point at, so Hibernate can’t generate a real FK and may require a different mapping approach (e.g., a Hibernate-specific “any”-style mapping, or redesigning to JOINED/SINGLE_TABLE so there is one parent table).
So if I try it anyway with standard annotations, what happens at schema-generation time vs runtime?

Schema generation: it may create a join column but won’t be able to add a valid foreign-key constraint (or it may fail DDL generation depending on dialect/settings). Runtime: if it boots, Hibernate can still persist a value, but loading/joining can be problematic or unsupported because it can’t unambiguously join to “the” Person table (since there isn’t one). In practice you often end up with either mapping exceptions at startup or queries that can’t be expressed as normal SQL joins.
Unrelated: I saw SQLite doesn’t even enforce foreign keys unless you enable them. Is that true?

Yes. In SQLite, foreign-key enforcement is controlled by a setting (commonly `PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;`). If it’s off, SQLite will accept FK declarations but won’t enforce them, which can hide integrity problems during development/testing.
And MySQL has some engine caveat too, right?
Right: in MySQL, foreign keys are enforced by InnoDB; older setups using MyISAM won’t enforce them. So you can see “FOREIGN KEY” in DDL but get no enforcement if the table engine doesn’t support it.
Cool—one more thing: in our schema, we actually turned off FK generation during dev, and a bunch of orphan rows slipped in before we noticed.

That can happen: without database-enforced foreign keys, Hibernate can still persist associations, but the DB won’t prevent rows pointing at missing parents. In dev, it’s common to disable FK DDL for speed, but it removes that safety net—so cleanup scripts or re-enabling constraints early helps.

Hey, quick correction: I kept saying “be concise” earlier because I was skimming, not because I always want short answers. When I’m actually learning this stuff, I prefer the longer, detailed explanations with examples.
Got it—so the earlier “be concise” requests were situational. For technical topics going forward, I’ll default to a more detailed explanation (and only go concise when you explicitly ask).
Exactly. Concise is only when I say so; otherwise give me the full context.
